plainMeaning: This verse names four great souls to call to mind and honour as the day begins — Jambū, Prince Nemi, Vajra, and Gautamasvāmī.
recitation: eka jambū jagajāṇīe, bījā nemakumāra. trījā vayara vakhāṇīe, cothā gautamasvāma.

wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. eka 'one', here used ordinally ('first') as the roll-call opens.
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with bījā / trījā / cothā to number the four exemplars.

wordByWord.etymology: Proper name; Jambūsvāmī, traditionally the last to attain kevala-jñāna in this era.
wordByWord.notes: First in the list — the closing omniscient of the present avasarpiṇī.

wordByWord.gloss: let us know (him) throughout the world; (he) is renowned in the world
wordByWord.etymology: jaga (< Skt. jagat 'world') + jāṇīe (< Prakrit/Skt. root jñā 'to know'); vernacular 1st-pl. hortative/optative.
wordByWord.notes: Readable either as an exhortation ('let us know him world-over') or passively ('is world-renowned').

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal from bīja 'second' (< Skt. dvitīya).
Nema (< Skt. Nemi / Ariṣṭanemi) + kumāra 'prince', recalling the bridegroom-prince who renounced for ascetic life.
wordByWord.notes: 'kumāra' evokes Nemi as the young prince who turned back from his wedding to renounce.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal from trīja 'third' (< Skt. tṛtīya).
Apabhraṁśa/Prakrit vayara < Skt. vajra; the ācārya celebrated for his vidyās and labdhis.
wordByWord.notes: 'vayar' is the vernacular reflex of vajra.

wordByWord.etymology: From vakhāṇvuṁ 'to praise, expound' (< Skt. vyākhyāna 'exposition'); vernacular 1st-pl. hortative.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberately rhymes and parallels jagajāṇīe — 'to know' answered by 'to praise'.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal from cotha 'fourth' (< Skt. caturtha).
Gautama + svāma (< Skt. svāmin 'lord, master'); the final, climactic name.
wordByWord.notes: The book spells the final syllable -svāma (without long ī), apparently metri causa; standard form Gautamasvāmī.

literalTranslation: The first, Jambū — let us know him throughout the world; the second, Prince Nemi. The third, Vayar (Vajra) — let us extol him; the fourth, Gautamasvāmī.
idiomaticTranslation: First, let the whole world know Jambū; second, Prince Nemi. Third, let us sing the praises of Vajra; and fourth — Gautamasvāmī.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where verse 1 blessed its exemplars in formal Sanskrit, this dohā switches into warm vernacular and turns benediction into a numbered roll-call of remembrance (smaraṇa): one, two, three, four. The counting is not idle — it is a mnemonic cadence, the way a devotee tells beads of names at first light. Notice whom the verse gathers: Jambū, the last omniscient of this age; Nemi, a full Tīrthaṅkara (the 22nd); Vajra, an ācārya famed for vidyās and attainments; and Gautama, Mahāvīra's foremost disciple. The list deliberately spans the whole spiritual hierarchy — a Tīrthaṅkara, a kevalī, a great ācārya, a gaṇadhara — so that to remember these four is to embrace the entire lineage in miniature. The verse also turns on two paired verbs: jagajāṇīe ('let us know') and vakhāṇīe ('let us extol'). Knowing and praising are the two devotional acts the dohā asks of us — first recognise these souls, then voice their glory. Both verbs are 1st-person plural ('let us'), drawing the reciter into a shared, communal act rather than a private one. Above all, the ordering is purposeful: the count climbs to its fourth and final name, Gautamasvāmī — and there it stops, handing the reciter directly into the next two verses, which dwell on Gautama alone. This verse is thus the hinge of the garland: it moves us from the collective blessing of verse 1 to the single-pointed Gautama-devotion that follows.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse embodies smaraṇa — auspicious remembrance — as a recognised devotional discipline: invoking liberated and exemplary souls to orient the mind toward their virtues. Its choice of four figures quietly affirms the unbroken śruta-paramparā (transmission lineage): the teaching passes from Tīrthaṅkara (Nemi) through gaṇadhara (Gautama) and on through great ācāryas (Vajra) down to the last omniscient of the era (Jambū). Reverence is owed not to Tīrthaṅkaras alone but to the whole chain of those who realised and carried the dharma.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at the close of the dawn rite, the verse models a simple morning practice: name and bring to mind a few great souls before stepping into the day, letting their example set the tone. The reciter does both things the verse asks — knows (recognises) and extols (voices) — turning bare names into living inspiration.

crossReferences.text: Maṅgalaṁ Bhagavān Vīro… (verse 1 of this same Maṅgalācaraṇa, naming Vīra, Gautama, Sthūlabhadra as auspicious)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Bharahesar sajjhāy (the Rāi litany of exemplary monks and satīs remembered by name)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Jambuswami — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Jambū as Jambūsvāmī, traditionally the last to attain kevala-jñāna in the present era.

sources.title: Neminatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identification of 'Nemkumar' as Neminātha (Nemi/Ariṣṭanemi), the 22nd Tīrthaṅkara, the renouncing prince.

sources.title: Gautama Swami — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Gautamasvāmī (Indrabhūti Gautama), chief gaṇadhara of Mahāvīra, as the climactic fourth name.

sources.title: Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Gujarati dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Vernacular/Prakrit verb roots underlying jāṇīe (jñā, 'to know') and the reflex vayar < vajra.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots behind vakhāṇīe (vyākhyāna 'exposition/praise') and svāma (svāmin 'lord').

notes: Verse 2 is a classical-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa-flavoured dohā; readings follow the book verbatim. The verbs jagajāṇīe and vakhāṇīe are vernacular 1st-person-plural forms best read as hortatives ('let us know', 'let us praise'), though a passive reading ('is renowned', 'is praised') is also defensible. The identifications follow the conventional reading given in the preface — 'Nemkumar' as Neminātha (22nd Tīrthaṅkara) and 'Vayar' as Vajrasvāmī; should the book carry a pāṭhāntara (variant reading), that variant governs. The book prints the final name as 'ગૌતમસ્વામ' (gautamasvāma, without long final ī), preserved verbatim and likely shortened metri causa; the standard form is Gautamasvāmī. No individual author is named in the book for this verse.
